content.text: Fundamentals of Stream Processing in Python Data Pipelines

Modern data systems rarely work on static snapshots. Events happen continuously — user clicks, sensor readings, financial transactions, log lines — and the systems built to handle them must process data as it arrives, not hours later. Stream processing is the discipline of doing exactly that: ingesting, transforming, and acting on data in motion, record by record or in small windows of time, with latency measured in milliseconds to seconds rather than minutes or hours.

is a data processing paradigm where computation is applied to data continuously as it flows through a system, rather than collecting it all first and then processing it in one large batch. Each unit of data — called an
— is processed shortly after it arrives.

is the end-to-end system that moves data from a source (a Kafka topic, a WebSocket, a sensor, an API), applies transformations and enrichments, and delivers results to a sink (a database, a dashboard, another queue, or an alert system).
The business value of stream processing comes from

. A fraud detection system that flags a suspicious transaction two seconds after it happens can prevent the charge. One that runs nightly reports cannot. The same logic applies to real-time recommendations, live inventory management, infrastructure alerting, and anything where stale data produces worse outcomes.

Python occupies a strategic role here. It is not the fastest language for raw throughput, but it is the language of data engineering, machine learning, and scripting. Understanding stream processing in Python means understanding how Python-native tools (generators,
) interact with large-scale distributed systems like Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, and Apache Spark Structured Streaming.
Every piece of data in a stream is an
— a self-contained, timestamped fact about something that happened. Events are immutable. Once a user clicked a button at

# A typical event represented as a Python dict
event = {
    "event_id": "a3f9c1d2",
    "event_type": "page_view",
    "user_id": 9012,
    "page": "/product/42",
    "timestamp": "2024-06-01T14:32:01Z",
    "session_id": "s-88xy"
}
(a compact binary format with a schema). Avro is preferred in high-throughput production systems because it is smaller and enforces a schema contract.
is where data enters the pipeline. Common sources:
– The dominant distributed event log. Producers write events to named
, consumers read from those topics. Topics are split into
– Real-time bidirectional connections, used for live data feeds.
– Change Data Capture reads the database write-ahead log as a stream of insert/update/delete events (via Debezium).
Dashboards (via APIs or time-series databases like InfluxDB)
Object storage (S3, GCS) for micro-batch archival

[Source]  →  [Ingestion Layer]  →  [Processing Layer]  →  [Sink]
  Kafka          Consumer              Transform              DB
  Socket         Generator             Filter              Kafka
  HTTP           AsyncIO               Aggregate            API
is typically a Kafka consumer or an async HTTP listener. The
is where Python logic lives — filtering, enriching, aggregating, joining. The
is a writer to a downstream system.
has a definitive end — a CSV file, a database snapshot, last month's logs. Batch jobs process bounded data.

has no end — a Kafka topic keeps receiving events indefinitely. Streaming systems process unbounded data. This is the fundamental technical challenge: you cannot wait for all the data before starting computation, because "all the data" never arrives.
5. Time in Streams — The Most Critical Concept
Time is where stream processing becomes genuinely hard. There are three distinct notions of time:
— When the event actually happened, recorded in the event's own timestamp field. This is what business logic usually cares about. A click happened at
— When the event arrived at the messaging system (e.g., when Kafka received it). Close to event time if the producer is fast, but can differ if there is network delay.
— When the pipeline processed the event. Always later than event time, sometimes by seconds, sometimes by hours (if the pipeline was down).
The gap between event time and processing time is called
. A mobile app that was offline for 20 minutes and then reconnects will flood the pipeline with 20 minutes worth of events, all with old timestamps. The pipeline must handle this gracefully.

is the streaming system's best guess about how far behind real-time the event data is. It is a threshold that says: "I believe I have seen all events with timestamps up to time T. Events older than T that arrive now are considered late."
Formally, if the current watermark is
is the configured allowed lateness (e.g., 5 minutes).
Watermarks let the system make progress on time-based aggregations without waiting forever for late stragglers.

# Flink PyFlink watermark strategy
watermark_strategy = WatermarkStrategy \
    .for_bounded_out_of_orderness(Duration.of_seconds(30)) \
    .with_timestamp_assigner(MyTimestampAssigner())
Because streams are infinite, you cannot aggregate "everything." Instead, you aggregate over
— finite slices of the infinite stream, defined by time or count.
Non-overlapping, fixed-size windows. Each event belongs to exactly one window.
Use case: "Count page views per minute."
Overlapping windows. Each event can belong to multiple windows.
A 5-minute window sliding every 1 minute means at any given second, an event is counted in up to 5 windows. Use case: "Rolling 5-minute average, updated every minute."
Dynamic windows that group events by periods of activity, separated by gaps of inactivity.
Use case: "Group all clicks in a user session, where a session ends after 30 minutes of no activity."
Windows defined by number of events rather than time.
Use case: "Process in micro-batches of 100 events."
— the result for the current event depends on previously seen events.
Counting events requires remembering the previous count.
Detecting a sequence of events (login → purchase → refund) requires memory of past events per user.
Aggregating over a time window requires buffering events until the window closes.
operations (filter, map, type conversion) are trivially parallelizable.
operations require care about where state is stored, how it is partitioned, and what happens when a node fails.
In large-scale systems, state is stored in fast embedded stores like
(used by Kafka Streams and Flink) — an on-disk key-value store that fits in the processing node but persists across crashes.
When a consumer processes events slower than the producer emits them, the queue of unprocessed events grows. This is called
. Without control mechanisms, the queue grows unboundedly and memory is exhausted.
Proper streaming systems handle backpressure by:
queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=1000)  # blocks producer when full
), signaling the producer to slow down.
In a distributed system, failures happen — network partitions, crashes, restarts. The pipeline must define what guarantees it offers:
— Events are delivered zero or one time. If the system crashes, some events may be lost. No duplicates. Fast and simple.

— Events are delivered one or more times. If the system crashes and retries, duplicates may occur. This is the default for most Kafka consumers. Requires idempotent sinks (writing the same event twice must not corrupt results).

— Events are processed precisely once, even through failures. The hardest guarantee. Requires distributed transactions or idempotent state management. Kafka supports exactly-once semantics (EOS) between Kafka-to-Kafka operations using transactional producers.
How to Build Streaming Pipelines in Python
Pattern 1: Generator-Based Pipeline (Pure Python, Small Scale)
Python generators are a natural fit for streaming because they are lazy — they produce one item at a time, on demand. This is exactly the push/pull model of stream processing.
This pipeline is lazy, memory-efficient, and composable. Each stage only processes one event at a time. The entire chain fits in constant memory regardless of how long it runs.
When the source or sink involves I/O (HTTP requests, database writes, network sockets),
is essential for handling many concurrent events without blocking.

import asyncio
import aiohttp
import json
import time
import random

# Async source: read from a WebSocket or HTTP endpoint
async def async_event_source(queue: asyncio.Queue) -> None:
    """Simulate events arriving from an external source."""
    while True:
        event = {
            "user_id": random.randint(1, 1000),
            "action": random.choice(["click", "purchase", "view"]),
            "timestamp": time.time()
        }
        await queue.put(event)
        await asyncio.sleep(0.05)  # 20 events/second

# Async processor: enrich each event (e.g., async DB lookup)
async def async_processor(input_queue: asyncio.Queue, output_queue: asyncio.Queue) -> None:
    while True:
        event = await input_queue.get()
        # Simulate async enrichment (e.g., fetch user profile from DB)
        await asyncio.sleep(0.01)
        event["enriched"] = True
        event["user_tier"] = "premium" if event["user_id"] % 2 == 0 else "standard"
        await output_queue.put(event)
        input_queue.task_done()

# Async sink: write processed events
async def async_sink(queue: asyncio.Queue) -> None:
    while True:
        event = await queue.get()
        print(f"Processed: user={event['user_id']} action={event['action']} tier={event['user_tier']}")
        queue.task_done()

# Run the pipeline with multiple concurrent processors
async def run_pipeline():
    source_queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=500)
    processed_queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=500)

    # Spin up 4 concurrent processors for parallelism
    processors = [
        asyncio.create_task(async_processor(source_queue, processed_queue))
        for _ in range(4)
    ]

    await asyncio.gather(
        async_event_source(source_queue),
        async_sink(processed_queue),
        *processors
    )

asyncio.run(run_pipeline())
on each queue provides natural backpressure — if the processor is slow, the source queue fills up and the producer is forced to wait.
For production, Kafka is the standard backbone. The
library provides a high-performance Python client backed by

from confluent_kafka import Consumer, Producer, KafkaError
import json
import signal
import sys

# --- Producer (Source side, sending events to Kafka) ---
def create_producer():
    return Producer({
        'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092',
        'acks': 'all',                    # wait for all replicas
        'enable.idempotence': True,        # exactly-once at producer level
        'retries': 5,
        'retry.backoff.ms': 200
    })

def publish_event(producer: Producer, topic: str, event: dict) -> None:
    producer.produce(
        topic=topic,
        key=str(event['user_id']).encode('utf-8'),  # partition by user_id
        value=json.dumps(event).encode('utf-8'),
        on_delivery=lambda err, msg: print(f"Delivery error: {err}") if err else None
    )
    producer.poll(0)  # trigger delivery callbacks non-blocking

# --- Consumer (Processing side, reading from Kafka) ---
def create_consumer(group_id: str):
    return Consumer({
        'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092',
        'group.id': group_id,
        'auto.offset.reset': 'earliest',
        'enable.auto.commit': False,       # manual commit for at-least-once control
        'max.poll.interval.ms': 300000,
    })

def run_consumer_pipeline():
    consumer = create_consumer(group_id='my-pipeline-v1')
    consumer.subscribe(['raw-events'])

    running = True
    def shutdown(signum, frame):
        nonlocal running
        running = False
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, shutdown)

    try:
        while running:
            msg = consumer.poll(timeout=1.0)
            if msg is None:
                continue
            if msg.error():
                if msg.error().code() == KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
                    continue  # end of partition, not a real error
                raise Exception(f"Kafka error: {msg.error()}")

            # Deserialize
            event = json.loads(msg.value().decode('utf-8'))

            # Process
            processed = transform(event)

            # Write to sink (another Kafka topic, database, etc.)
            write_to_sink(processed)

            # Commit AFTER successful processing (at-least-once guarantee)
            consumer.commit(asynchronous=False)

    finally:
        consumer.close()

def transform(event: dict) -> dict:
    event['processed_at'] = time.time()
    event['amount_usd'] = round(event.get('amount', 0) * 1.0, 2)
    return event

def write_to_sink(event: dict) -> None:
    # In production: write to PostgreSQL, Elasticsearch, etc.
    print(f"Sink: {event}")
Pattern 4: Windowed Aggregation in Pure Python
Implementing a tumbling window counter without a framework:

import time
from collections import defaultdict
from typing import Iterator

def tumbling_window_count(
    stream: Iterator[dict],
    window_size_seconds: float = 60.0
) -> Iterator[dict]:
    """
    Aggregates events into tumbling time windows.
    Emits a summary dict at the end of each window.
    """
    window_start = time.time()
    counts = defaultdict(int)

    for event in stream:
        now = time.time()

        # Check if the current window has elapsed
        if now - window_start >= window_size_seconds:
            # Emit the window result
            yield {
                "window_start": window_start,
                "window_end": now,
                "counts": dict(counts)
            }
            # Reset for next window
            window_start = now
            counts = defaultdict(int)

        # Accumulate event in current window
        event_type = event.get("action", "unknown")
        counts[event_type] += 1
Pattern 5: Using Faust (Kafka Streams for Python)
is a Python stream processing library built on
and Kafka, inspired by Kafka Streams (Java). It provides high-level abstractions for topics, agents, and tables (stateful key-value stores).

import faust
from datetime import datetime

app = faust.App(
    'clickstream-processor',
    broker='kafka://localhost:9092',
    value_serializer='json',
)

# Define topic schemas
class ClickEvent(faust.Record, serializer='json'):
    user_id: int
    page: str
    timestamp: float

class PageViewCount(faust.Record, serializer='json'):
    page: str
    count: int

# Define topics
clicks_topic = app.topic('user-clicks', value_type=ClickEvent)
page_counts_topic = app.topic('page-view-counts', value_type=PageViewCount)

# Persistent stateful table (backed by RocksDB via Kafka changelog topics)
page_view_table = app.Table(
    'page-views',
    default=int,
    key_type=str,
    value_type=int
)

# Agent: processes each event from the source topic
@app.agent(clicks_topic)
async def process_clicks(clicks):
    async for click in clicks:
        # Update stateful count
        page_view_table[click.page] += 1

        # Emit to output topic
        await page_counts_topic.send(
            key=click.page,
            value=PageViewCount(
                page=click.page,
                count=page_view_table[click.page]
            )
        )

# Timer: emit totals every 60 seconds
@app.timer(interval=60.0)
async def emit_summary():
    for page, count in page_view_table.items():
        print(f"{datetime.now()}: {page} → {count} total views")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.main()
Faust handles consumer group management, offset commits, changelog compaction, and fault tolerance automatically.
Processing in event-time order when you receive out-of-order events.

Mobile clients, IoT sensors, and distributed producers frequently deliver events out of order. Never assume the event with the highest timestamp arrived last. Always use explicit watermarking and allowed lateness.
Committing Kafka offsets before writing to the sink.
If you commit the offset and then the sink write fails, the event is silently lost (at-most-once). Always write to the sink first, confirm success, then commit the offset.
allows unlimited growth. Under load spikes, this causes OOM crashes. Always cap queue sizes and handle
Storing large state in-process without TTL.
Session tracking, deduplication sets, and counters grow indefinitely unless you expire old entries. A dedup set for
values will consume all available memory if events accumulate for weeks.

# Anti-pattern: unbounded dedup set
seen_ids = set()

# Better: use a time-bounded structure or Redis with TTL
import redis
r = redis.Redis()
def is_duplicate(event_id: str) -> bool:
    key = f"seen:{event_id}"
    # Returns True if key already existed
    return not r.set(key, 1, nx=True, ex=3600)  # 1 hour TTL
Using processing time for business logic instead of event time.
"How many orders in the last hour?" answered with processing time will give wrong answers when there is pipeline lag. Always timestamp events at the source.
Blocking I/O inside an async function.
blocks the entire event loop. Use

# Wrong
async def process(event):
    response = requests.get(f"http://api/user/{event['user_id']}")  # BLOCKS!

# Correct
async def process(event, session: aiohttp.ClientSession):
    async with session.get(f"http://api/user/{event['user_id']}") as response:
        return await response.json()
Design for idempotency at the sink.
Since at-least-once delivery means duplicates are possible, your sink writer should use upsert logic (insert or update based on a unique key), not blind inserts.
Key Kafka messages by the entity they describe.
ensures all events for the same user land on the same partition and are processed in order. Unkeyed messages are distributed round-robin and lose ordering guarantees.
Use dead-letter queues (DLQ) for poison events.
Events that fail processing repeatedly should be routed to a separate DLQ topic for manual inspection rather than blocking the main pipeline.
Each function should do one thing (filter, enrich, validate, aggregate). This makes testing straightforward — unit-test each stage with a list of test events.
Consumer lag (how far behind the consumer is from the latest Kafka offset) is the most critical health metric for a streaming pipeline. Tools: Kafka's
, Burrow (LinkedIn's open-source lag monitor), or Prometheus + Grafana with the Kafka exporter.
Schema evolution via Avro + Schema Registry.
When event schemas change (new fields, renamed fields), Avro with Confluent Schema Registry enforces backward/forward compatibility, preventing pipeline breakage during deployments.
The throughput of a Python streaming pipeline depends heavily on where time is spent:
— If processing requires heavy computation per event (e.g., running an ML model), Python's GIL becomes a bottleneck. Use
to spawn parallel workers, or offload to a faster runtime (Rust, Java) using the pipeline as an orchestrator.
— If processing requires database lookups, API calls, or cache reads,
with concurrent tasks achieves high throughput because the event loop can handle thousands of concurrent I/O waits with a single thread.

— The number of partitions in a Kafka topic is the maximum parallelism for consumers in the same group. With 16 partitions, you can have at most 16 consumer instances processing in parallel. Design partition counts with future scale in mind (they cannot be reduced without data migration).
— Writing to a database one event at a time is extremely slow. Batch events into groups of 100–1000 before flushing to the sink:
— If every event requires a user profile lookup, caching profiles in memory (with TTL) reduces database load by orders of magnitude:

from functools import lru_cache
import asyncio

# For async: use a dict with manual TTL management
_user_cache = {}
_cache_ttl = 300  # 5 minutes

async def get_user_profile(user_id: int, db_pool) -> dict:
    now = time.monotonic()
    if user_id in _user_cache:
        profile, cached_at = _user_cache[user_id]
        if now - cached_at < _cache_ttl:
            return profile

    profile = await db_pool.fetchrow("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", user_id)
    _user_cache[user_id] = (dict(profile), now)
    return dict(profile)
Simpler setup, lower throughput than confluent-kafka
Kafka Streams-equivalent in Python, with stateful tables
Complex event processing, windowing, large-scale stateful jobs
Existing Spark infrastructure, ML pipelines, SQL-based streaming
Building custom async pipelines, consuming HTTP/WebSocket sources
New tool; Rust-backed performance with Python API, good for ML

Python's streaming libraries are generally I/O-bound and appropriate for pipelines doing transformation, enrichment, and routing. For truly compute-intensive stateful aggregations at very high scale, PyFlink or Spark provide significantly more power.

— Production Kafka clusters require SASL authentication (username/password or mTLS certificates) and TLS encryption in transit. Never connect to Kafka without TLS in any environment where data is sensitive.
— Deserializing arbitrary JSON or pickle data from untrusted sources is dangerous. Never use
for cross-system event serialization. Prefer JSON with schema validation, or Avro with Schema Registry enforcement.

— Each pipeline stage should use a distinct consumer group ID. Multiple pipelines sharing a group ID will split partitions between them, causing each pipeline to silently process only a subset of events.
to flush in-flight events, commit outstanding offsets, and close connections cleanly before exit.

import signal
import asyncio

shutdown_event = asyncio.Event()

def handle_shutdown(signum, frame):
    print("Shutdown signal received, draining pipeline...")
    shutdown_event.set()

signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, handle_shutdown)
signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, handle_shutdown)

async def run():
    while not shutdown_event.is_set():
        # process events
        await asyncio.sleep(0.01)
    # cleanup
    await flush_pending_events()
    consumer.close()

— For critical pipelines (financial transactions, billing), implement idempotent writes using the event's unique ID as the primary key, and use database transactions to atomically write the event and commit the Kafka offset:

async def exactly_once_write(event: dict, conn, consumer):
    async with conn.transaction():
        await conn.execute(
            "INSERT INTO events (id, data) VALUES ($1, $2) ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING",
            event['event_id'],
            json.dumps(event)
        )
        # Offset is committed inside the DB transaction — either both succeed or both fail
        await store_offset_in_db(conn, topic=msg.topic(), partition=msg.partition(), offset=msg.offset())
Stream processing treats data as an infinite, continuous flow of events, enabling real-time computation with latency in the milliseconds-to-seconds range rather than hours.
Time semantics (event time vs. processing time, watermarks, allowed lateness) are the central technical challenge of streaming — always use event time for business logic.
Windows (tumbling, sliding, session) are the mechanism for aggregating unbounded streams into finite, actionable results.
Python offers a spectrum of tools: generator pipelines for simple in-process streaming,
for I/O-heavy concurrent pipelines, Faust for Kafka-backed stateful streaming, and PyFlink for large-scale distributed jobs.
Delivery semantics (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once) must be chosen deliberately; at-least-once with idempotent sinks is the practical default for most systems.
Backpressure must be built into every pipeline — bounded queues, flow control, and consumer lag monitoring are non-negotiable for production stability.
State management, schema evolution, graceful shutdown, and security (TLS, SASL, no pickle) separate a prototype pipeline from a production-grade one.
